suggestions.text: Write down one moment from today that you may want to remember tomorrow.
If it feels natural, note one thing you appreciated today without forcing a positive interpretation.
Choose one brief, familiar wind-down activity before your next usual sleep period.
Notice whether keeping a similar wind-down time feels supportive over the next few days.
Consider one short pause away from the current task before returning to it.
At the end of the task, name one boundary that marks work or study as finished for now.
If it feels welcome, send a simple message to someone you trust.
Consider asking someone you trust for a brief check-in rather than solving everything alone.
If comfortable, take a few unforced slower breaths and stop if it feels unpleasant.
Pause and name a few neutral things you can see, hear, or feel around you.
If comfortable, briefly soften one area where you notice ordinary muscle tension.

claimCards.displayClaim: Structured reflective or expressive writing has been studied as a way of organizing experiences and noticing personally meaningful themes.
claimCards.limitations: Writing approaches, populations, and outcomes vary; this does not establish what an individual reflection means.

claimCards.displayClaim: Research on rest and sleep health includes regular routines, relaxation practices, and other behavioral approaches.
claimCards.limitations: These are general study contexts, not personalized sleep advice or an explanation of an individual's current state.

claimCards.displayClaim: Work-break research examines how brief pauses and recovery practices relate to wellbeing and task experience.
claimCards.limitations: Effects vary by task, person, and setting; this does not prove that workload caused an individual's feelings.
